meta.title: This House believes that, over the next decade, value will accrue to applications rather than L1s.

transcript:
Brian Rudick: Hey everybody, thanks so much for being here. I am Brian Rudick, I am the Chief Strategy Officer at Upexi. We did one of the very first SPACs and we could have done it on any token, we chose to be underpinned by Solana because we believe very strongly that Solana is the endgame winning high-performance blockchain. All of us on stage believe very strongly that Solana is undervalued. So we actually tweaked this debate a bit and we'll be talking about where value will accrue over the long run, whether that's to the application layer or whether that's to the L1. Mike and I are on the side of that accruing to the applications. There are four key reasons for us. Number one, blockchains and blockspace are abundant. There are already hundreds of high-performance blockchains. It's easy to spin one up. And all the blockchains that already exist are working on increasing throughput. Also, these blockchains compete on cost. So it is a race to zero on fees. And there are additional constructs that reduce the power of L1s. These are interoperability protocols, the ability of successful apps to actually create their own chain. And so increasing commoditization of blockspace means that value capture for the L1 will be low. Number two, applications are closest to the user and therefore in the best position to monetize that. Apps can better differentiate with greater moats around UI/UX, content, user base, IP, and much more. And there are various initiatives to push value capture more toward the apps that are in progress. These are things like intent-based architecture and application-controlled execution. They say he who owns the user owns the market. Number three, this is actually what we're seeing in other areas of technology, and particularly when you're dealing with commoditized or open source infrastructure. The internet protocols are a great example. TCP/IP, HTTP, SMTP, DNS, broadband networks, they're mostly commoditized and low margin infrastructure. And it's the web applications that are built on top that capture trillions of dollars. Linux and open source stacks versus SaaS companies are another example. If the underlying layer is commoditized or open source, value tends to accrue to the applications built on top. And lastly, this is actually what we're seeing on Solana. So over the last 30 days, Solana application revenue was 115 million versus for the network it was just a small fraction of this. So this is already happening and it's hard to understand why this would reverse.

Cosmo Jiang: Hey everyone. Nice to see everyone here. I'm Cosmo, I'm General Partner at Pantera Capital and Director at Solana Company, HGT. We're here to argue that applications... that the L1 will accrue more value than the applications over time. I think, you know, one thing to think about is that applications will... and I agree with Brian here, will always create enormous value. Some will be very valuable businesses. But the idea that this happens instead of value or less... more than the value that accrues to the L1 is false. In every crypto application, applications monetize users, they monetize activity, but layer ones monetize all that activity plus the monetary capture on the base layer. Every application, no matter what it is, has to live on an L1. It has to pay rent to an L1. When you think about what apps are, apps are like stores at a mall. Whereas, you know, they pay rent, they benefit from the traffic and the users that exist in the mall. Well, the mall owner is the one that is more permanent and actually captures the value on... that collects rent from all the application owners and all the stores.

Mike Dudas: Point of clarity. When you say that applications pay rent to the L1, what exactly do you mean? I do hear that term a lot and I'm not sure what rent actually means in this context.

Cosmo Jiang: Yeah, in this context it means transaction fees. So the applications need to, you know, eventually they need to pay rent to the L1. Often times it's, you know, today the setup is that the users pay for it, but, you know, at some point in the future perhaps applications need to subsidize that. Which maybe gets to my next point, which is that, you know, I actually love that blockchain is open source, that everything is composable. Well, one of the side effects of that is that there's a ton of application at the application layer. I'd argue that L1s have a much higher moat because they are infrastructure on which all the applications live. And I love all the application developers out there, I think you guys are all doing a great job, but the competition there is much more fierce than it is at the L1 layer. And eventually I'd expect that, you know, margins compress over time at the application layer, but layer one application revenue or their ability to capture value will always stay constant. The last thing I'd say is that, look, this is in particular true I think on high-performance chains like Solana. They're explicitly designed to maximize shared state, minimize app layer rent. And so as apps get cheaper and less competitive... and more competitive with each other over time, you know, the base asset actually becomes more valuable and it captures all the value from today's applications, tomorrow's applications, and all the applications in the future, whereas applications have to fight the day-to-day knife fight battle of being out there every day. So I guess to conclude, I think there's going to be a ton of value created across the ecosystem. It's not about a, you know, who... it's not a zero-sum game. It is that the whole ecosystem will create a lot of value across both apps and the L1. But the L1's value capture and the durability of that will be much higher over time.

Mike Dudas: All right. So I guess I have 10 minutes because you guys didn't take all your time. So I'll go... I mean I can talk all day. I am absolutely certain that you do. So, I'm going to first I think talk, speak to some of the points that you made. So one was that, you know, margins compress at the app layer over time. And I think in the history of technology, certainly over the last, you know, call it 30 years, it actually doesn't seem to be the case. If you look at, you know, basically the largest right now, the Mag 7, the ones that are, you know, sort of pure internet companies like Google, you know, sort of started out, call it as a platform that sent folks out, you know, elsewhere and took a little bit of a take rate. But actually over time, they have now come back to being a set of applications themselves, including Gemini, and then are now monetizing their users directly in nearly every vertical where they actually used to send out their users to other folks, other providers in the ecosystem. And I think when the platforms start doing that, it kind of tells you, hey, being at the app layer is actually the place where you can sort of capture a significant amount of the value. I would say, you know, in the sort of ads and attention space, it's been pretty clear that Facebook's pretty much bought up all distribution, sort of plugged it into their overarching, you know, ads monetization, eyeballs, data targeting system. And that they and the entire social and attention space where people spending most of their time online are monetizing at the app layer versus at a protocol layer. These aren't perfect analogies. I'm waiting for Kyle to buzz me and say that.

Kyle Samani: My mic... like, yeah, Facebook and Google make all this money by controlling attention, but like the underlying infrastructure... I mean if you look at the last two years in software and tech, the people making the most... stock prices going up the most are all of the infrastructure companies. Broadcom, NVIDIA, a bunch of the whole chip supply chain, Himax, all those guys. So like as the apps get used more, it's actually right now the infrastructure companies are actually the ones making more money.

Mike Dudas: So on an absolute basis, but on sort of a velocity of change basis, the app layer companies are making just an incredible amount of money as well. And I think both of the earlier speakers did say there is some level of symbiosis between sort of the platforms and in your example sort of the super scalers making money and the folks who build on top of them. Let me actually get back to one other example. Apple I actually think is the best example. I like analogies. Please buzz soon. Apple's, you know, one of the greatest platform businesses and sort of we can almost equate it to sort of like a mobile... it's not a layer one, but it's just like the place everybody goes, it's the mobile underlying brand. Their take rate is 30% on any app in the App Store. And then, you know, they have take rate in other places on payments and things like that. But the majority of the revenue is going to the apps built on top of the Apple App Store. And many would argue Android's take rate is much lower, it's I think sub 10%. And many would argue that Apple's 70%... sorry, 30% is too high. I'm going to run through just my individual points, but first I will answer your point of contention. Is that number three?

Kyle Samani: Second. But like your 30% thing is actually an argument in favor of infrastructure because the App Store is not an app, it's infrastructure they provide to developers so that people can get apps.

Mike Dudas: I would argue that it's directly... I mean now we're getting into like monopoly and tying, but it's like directly tied to what I would call the underlying L1. And they extract the rent. So the argument here is who makes more money relatively, in aggregate the app developers do. The 70%. Okay. Interesting argument, but yes. That was my argument. So, the next piece is if blockspace is valuable and it gets to the Apple thing, well then that means that, you know, everything on top of that blockspace is actually generating profit. As you mentioned, we've seen that over the past year on Solana and the app layer of Solana... I'm getting buzzed off, I got 40 seconds... has made, you know, significantly more than the underlying protocol. One argument that I've heard recently from Haseeb and others is this exponentials argument. Well exponentials, just like they work for L1s and for the underlying network layer, they also work for applications. Many of the fastest growing apps in the history of technology, Axiom, Pump, and a handful of others are crypto applications that are charging fees. And, you know what? I feel great about that. We're going to finish there. Thanks guys.

Kyle Samani: I agree. Oh good, I'm even green over there so we actually have green and purple. I like it. Awesome. Hi everyone, my name is Kyle Samani, I'm a Founder and Managing Partner at Multicoin Capital. You know, I think the whole debate between apps and the L1 is a little... it's framed... most of the discussion has been framed in the context of an analogy. And if you know me, you might know that I don't really like analogies. The challenge with crypto is that this infrastructure is there to support finance. And actually the majority of financial traffic in the world comes from bots. HFT firms, market makers, whatever. That's true in TradFi, it's true in crypto. That will always be true because bots can click buttons much faster than people can click buttons. And so, you know, when I think about like a world in which we have a million transactions per second living on Solana, which you know hopefully that's a few years away, I would guess 97 to 99% of that is coming from bots. And that means the bots are explicitly not paying rent to the applications because they're just, you know, hooking into the contracts directly on chain. I think the transaction gas fees they pay are already low now and I think they'll go effectively towards zero. But where they will pay is for priority access and MEV. And if you look today at the, I think whatever, three or four billion that has been paid to Solana stakers this year, it's coming from traders who are willing to pay for priority access. The beauty of AI, the beauty of the world is the world is a heterogeneous place that is unknown. And that means asset prices will always be volatile. And that means there will always be money to be made competing for priority access. No amount of AGI or ASI even is going to change that. And so I kind of think about the apps as like, their importance because you need people to come click buttons, you know, who are excited about stuff. But the bots are ultimately going to be the ones who drive the vast majority of traffic and pay for priority access.

Cosmo Jiang: Yeah, Cosmo, you want to add anything? Yeah, I mean I'd say there's also an important question of, you know, the statement says over the next decade. And, you know, I think one of the points you made is it's important to realize that the applications are changing over time. And that the durability of the bots interacting on the layer one is higher than the users using different applications.

Mike Dudas: So is the argument there that literally the apps effectively go away and that the majority of folks interacting with the chain in the future are interacting directly with chains than with sort of, call it applications and other front ends?

Kyle Samani: I mean, look, people are always going to want to click buttons. But, you know, right now the fees that most of these apps are charging is pretty egregious. Coinbase charges 150 bips. I mean Axiom charges I think 85 or something like that. I think Pump is in the same range if I recall. Those, you know, if JPMorgan charged 150 bips to trade US equities, Elizabeth Warren would be on TV demanding that he go to jail. Right? But in crypto I guess it's cool. Or at least it has been historically. I don't think that's going to be the case. So I do think you'll see dramatic margin compression on the app side over the next few years. Meanwhile the infrastructure side, the reality that trading firms are always going to compete, that can never go away. Yeah, I think that's the end of my argument boys.

Brian Rudick: I think it's your closing argument first.

Cosmo Jiang: Oh, we close first. Looks like some bots are voting for green team over here. Go bots go. Yeah, the bots need love. I have an airdrop for you if you vote green. All right, priority fees, I see it. I think a lot of the argument today has been about, and a lot of analogies have been made about platforms versus applications. I think what's really interesting is that in a crypto world, the platform, you know, the equivalent of Facebook, Apple, whoever, is actually the L1. And like a lot of the value capture at these large mega cap tech companies that we see today is at the infrastructure layer, not at the application layer. You know, it's that Google monetizes because they have a massive user base and massive brand value, but each of their individual applications don't actually monetize at the same level. And they actually rotate in and out. You look at the large language models today, I think there's a lot of question whether any of them will make money. And whether, you know, OpenAI, Anthropic, any of those models will make money. But it's very clear that the infrastructure provider will make money. History is also path dependent. In a world where, and you know, as we're looking here today over the next decade, it's clear that AI will proliferate across everything we do. AI is making code commoditized. And so more and more so we're going to see it's going to be easier to develop applications. Because it's easier to develop applications, the barrier to entry is lower, the cost of capital is lower, and therefore there's going to be more competition. I actually believe there will be more and more smaller, thinner, niche specific applications, which is good for users and good for the ecosystem and great for the L1, but less good for value capture for each individual application. Over the next decade, the leading apps will have changed hands multiple times, but the L1s will not. And that's why over the next decade, L1s will capture more value than any single application.

Brian Rudick: Bring it home Brian. Yeah, these are good points on both sides. I'll just repeat what our main four key arguments and then address some of the things that were said. So blockchains and blockspace are being commoditized. There are so many of them and it's a race to zero on the fee side. Applications are closest to the end user and therefore in the best position to monetize. It was brought up that folks will pay for priority access. It's already happening and we're actually seeing different constructs like ACE that is moving this much more toward the apps and their ability to capture it. We're already seeing the value accrue to the applications rather than the base infrastructure and particularly when it is on commoditized or open source infrastructure. It was brought up that NVIDIA is a good example and yes, they are capturing a lot of value, but I would argue that NVIDIA is a monopoly. There's very few that have their level of technology with respect to GPUs. Also Google was brought up too. I think that Google is better compared to an application and that application is built on the internet which captures little value. And then lastly we're already seeing this happen on Solana with the vast majority of the value going to the applications. I don't think there's any real reason why that will change over time. Thank you.

Moderator: Thank you. People need to take much stronger stances. 50/50. Unacceptable. It's unacceptable. We should vote. Should we do a robot fight? I heard it went not as well for some people yesterday. Oh, hurtful. Someone write a bot please. Okay. Ooh, this is close. Um, I think we're going to take... It is actually tied. Yeah. Final 10 seconds. If you haven't voted, please vote and we'll declare a winner. If we have a winner. Nine. Eight. Oh I like it. Seven. Six. Five. There we go. Four. Come on Carl. Call your friends. Three. Two. One. We'll end it there with declaring Pro the winner. Oh! When did they close it? Well, thank you. Thank you to both sides. Thank you all. Appreciate it. Thank you. Awesome. We're going to take two minutes, clear off the stage before we have some exciting product keynotes coming up. So we'll be right back.

enrichment.generated_query:
The provided transcript snippet does not contain information about a specific product being launched or a company launching such a product. The speakers are engaged in a debate about value accrual, not announcing new products. Therefore, Rules 1 and 2 cannot be fulfilled from the given text.

However, adhering to Rule 3 (Include 'Solana Breakpoint 2025') and Rule 4 (Output ONLY the query string), the most relevant query that can be formed based on the available information and the intent of the task is:

Solana Breakpoint 2025 official announcement blog technical docs

sources.title: Onchain x LI.FI: Solana Breakpoint Campaign
sources.snippet:
Get Started on Onchain Wallet

[Download Now](https://crypto.com/en/onchain)

[EVENTS](https://crypto.com/en/events) \|2D AGO

# Onchain x LI.FI: Solana Breakpoint Campaign

Win USDC rewards or exclusive Onchain merch

10 - 17 DEC, 2025UTC

To mark Solana Breakpoint 2025, Crypto.com Onchain has partnered with Li.Fi for a special week-long campaign featuring an in-person giveaway and a Solana on-chain trading challenge.

Whether you're attending Breakpoint in Abu Dhabi or participating online, there’s something for everyone! Complete two simple activities to claim exclusive merchandise or trade using Onchain to win from a 2,000 USDC prize pool.

**CAMPAIGN-SPECIFIC TERMS:**

**Campaign Period**

- **Activity 1: Meet & Selfie Challenge:** 10 December 2025, 00:00 UTC to 13 December 2025, 23:59 UTC
- **Activity 2: Solana Trading Challenge:** 10 December 2025, 00:00 UTC to 16 December 2025, 23:59 UTC

**Activity 1. Meet & Selfie (@Breakpoint)**

Join us at the Etihad Arena in Abu Dhabi! Meet the Onchain or Li.Fi team, complete a simple trade, and walk away with exclusive merchandise.

**How to Participate**

_If you wish to participate in the campaign, you may consider completing the following actions:_

- Find an Onchain Wallet or LI.FI team member at Breakpoint,
- Take a selfie with them,
- Post the selfie publicly on X (formerly Twitter),
 - Tag: @onchain\_wallet and @lifiprotocol
 - Include hashtag: #LIFIOnchain
- Download and open the Crypto.com Onchain Wallet, and
- Complete a trade using LI.FI in the Crypto Onchain Wallet app
 - Any chain, any amount, cross-chain or same-chain

**Reward**

- Choose from Onchain-branded swag (“Merch Reward”).
- Available to the first 40 verified and eligible users during the Campaign Period of the Activity 1.
- Merch Reward must be claimed on-site after staff confirmation.
- Limit of one Merch reward per user per day.

**Activity 2: Solana Trading Challenge**

Trade using Onchain Wallet’s LI.FI integration with Solana as part of the route. The top three traders by volume during the campaign will win USDC rewards.

**How to Participate**

_If you wish to participate in the campaign, you may consider completing the following actions:_

- Use Onchain Wallet’s LI.FI-powered swap feature
- Trade involving Solana
 - Examples: ETH to SOL, SOL to USDC, USDC on Solana
- No minimum amount required
- Only trades completed using LI.FI inside the Onchain Wallet will count
- All trades must be performed on Onchain Wallet within the Campaign Period of the Activity 2.

**Rewards**

- **1st Place:** 1,000 USDC
- **2nd Place:** 500 USDC
- **3rd Place:** 250 USDC
- **4th Place:** 150 USDC
- **5th place:** 100 USDC

Winners will be announced within 7 days after the Campaign Period of the Activity 2 ends. Rewards will be sent directly to the eligible wallet addresses on Solana within 7 days after the winner announcement of Activity 2.

**Eligibility and Rules**

- No signup required. Wallet activity and public posts are used to determine eligibility
- All actions must take place within the campaign windows listed above
- Each user is only eligible for one reward per track per day
- Suspicious activity such as wash trading or duplicate wallets will lead to disqualification
- In case of a tie in trading volume, the user who reached that volume first will win
- Onchain Wallet staff will verify on-site winners by checking trade history and social media posts
- All rewards are subject to verification and may be withheld if conditions are not met

### **Important Information**

- The Crypto.com Onchain Wallet is a standalone, non-custodial product and is not part of the regulated Crypto.com App or Exchange ecosystem.
- The Solana Breakpoint Campaign (“ **Campaign**”) is organised by Crypto.com Onchain Wallet and LI.FI, and is [available in select jurisdictions only](https://help.crypto.com/en/articles/4429907-crypto-com-onchain-wallet-swap-geo-restrictions). Participation is optional and subject to eligibility requirem

tags.impact_reason: The debate touches on a core discussion about the long-term value accrual within the Solana ecosystem, specifically regarding the balance between the L1 and dApps.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a monolithic, layered foundation as the Semantic Anchor to represent the L1 blockchain infrastructure, which the article describes as abundant, commoditized, and a 'race to zero'. The vibrant, glowing crystalline structures built on top represent the Applications, where the debate suggests value is now accruing. For the portrait version, I included the diegetic text 'FEES: 0%' stenciled onto the foundation itself, directly referencing the 'race to zero on fees' and visually stamping the L1 as a low-margin utility.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the dramatic textural contrast where an application meets the L1. The foreground is filled with the rough, pitted surface of the dark, monolithic concrete foundation. Emerging directly from this texture is the impossibly smooth, sharp corner of a polished obsidian structure, which glows with a powerful internal light. The light from the app catches the imperfections in the concrete, creating a high-contrast interplay of shadow and light, highlighting the difference between the raw infrastructure and the refined application.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot focusing on the dramatic textural contrast where an application meets the L1, composed for a 1:1 aspect ratio with central focus and tight framing, filling the frame with texture. The foreground is filled with the rough, deeply pitted surface of the dark, monolithic concrete foundation, revealing its geological cross-section-like layers and imperfections. Emerging directly from this texture is the impossibly smooth, sharp corner of a highly polished obsidian structure, which glows with a powerful internal light, casting sharp specular highlights on its reflective surface. The intense, volumetric light from the application catches the granular imperfections in the concrete, creating a high-contrast interplay of dramatic shadow and bright light rays, highlighting the profound difference between the raw, textured infrastructure and the refined, glowing application. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), cinematic lighting.

editorial.headline: Investors Bet on Apps Over L1s at Breakpoint Debate
editorial.standfirst: As blockspace becomes abundant and fees race to zero, institutional investors signal a departure from the 'Fat Protocol' thesis. A high-profile debate at Breakpoint 2025 reveals the market is now pricing user ownership over infrastructure dominance.
key_takeaways: The 'Pro-App' side won the audience vote, signaling a shift in investor sentiment away from L1 value accrual.
Brian Rudick (Upexi) revealed Solana applications generated $115 million in revenue over 30 days, dwarfing network fees.
Multicoin's Kyle Samani countered that AI agents and bots will drive L1 revenue through priority fees and MEV, not user interfaces.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The “Fat Protocol” thesis—the decade-long dogma that value settles at the base layer—took a public beating at Solana Breakpoint 2025. In a razor-thin vote, the audience sided with the view that applications, not Layer 1 blockchains, will capture the lion's share of value over the next decade. The logic is rooted in commoditization: as high-performance chains proliferate and throughput expands, blockspace becomes abundant, driving base-layer fees to zero.

**Brian Rudick** (CSO, Upexi) and **Mike Dudas** (6th Man Ventures) anchored their victory on the “smile curve” of history. Drawing parallels to the early internet, they argued that while protocols like TCP/IP became public goods, the empires (Google, Meta) were built by those who owned the user interface. Rudick pointed to hard data: in the last month alone, Solana applications generated **$115 million** in revenue, a figure that eclipses the network's own capture. The argument is that applications possess the pricing power and moats—UX, IP, and liquidity—that commoditized infrastructure lacks.

The counter-argument, led by **Pantera’s Cosmo Jiang** and **Multicoin’s Kyle Samani**, pivoted away from human users entirely. Samani argued that the future finance stack will be dominated by bots and AI agents, which don't care about UI but will pay premiums for speed. In this view, the L1 is not just a settlement layer but a marketplace for **MEV (Maximal Extractable Value)** and priority access—a durable rent that applications cannot replicate. Jiang likened apps to churning retail stores in a mall, where the L1 “landlord” remains the only constant winner.

Ultimately, the vote signals a maturing market. While infrastructure remains critical, the “build it and they will come” premium is fading. Investors are looking for the next “Magnificent Seven” at the application layer, betting that sticky users and proprietary data will outperform the race-to-the-bottom economics of raw blockspace.
